revision.text:
'''Kamakura-gu''' (鎌倉宮) is a [[Jinja (shrine)|shrine]] in the city of [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It was erected by the [[Emperor Meiji]] in [[1869]] to worship the spirit of [[Prince Morinaga]], who was imprisoned and later executed on its shrine in [[1335]].
Prince Morinaga had fought on the side of Imperial Rule in the 1330s, but was captured by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[1334]]. Ashikaga's younger brother [[Ashikaga Tadayoshi]] is said to have held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura shrine. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from the shrine in battle, he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. He was beheaded on [[July 23]] [[1335]].
The cave still exists today in the rockface behind the shrine, and is a tourist attraction. It is four meters deep and has an area of 12 square meters.
{{Japan-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines]]

Prince Morinaga had fought on the side of Imperial Rule in the 1330s, but was captured by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[1334]]. Ashikaga's younger brother [[Ashikaga Tadayoshi]] is said to have held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura shrine. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from the shrine in battle, he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. He was beheaded on [[July 23]] [[1335]].
The cave still exists today in the rockface behind the shrine, and is a tourist attraction. It is four meters deep and has an area of 12 square meters.

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Kamakura-gū | image = Kamakuragu Main Hall.jpg | width = | caption = The Haiden | type = | dedication = Prince Morinaga | founded = 1869 | closed = | founder = Meiji Emperor | priest = | address = 154 Nikaido, Kamakura, Kanagawa 248–0002 | phone = 0467-22-0318 | website = http://www.kamakuraguu.jp/ }} {{Nihongo|'''Kamakura-gū'''|鎌倉宮|}} is a [[Jinja (shrine)|shrine]] in the city of [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It was erected by the [[Emperor Meiji]] in [[1869]] to worship the spirit of [[Prince Morinaga]], who was imprisoned and later executed on its shrine in [[1335]]. It is also called {{nihongo|Ōtōnomiya or Daitōnomiya|大塔宮}}.
Prince Morinaga had fought on the side of Imperial Rule in the 1330s, but was captured by [[Ashikaga Takauji]] in [[1334]]. Ashikaga's younger brother [[Ashikaga Tadayoshi]] is said to have held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura shrine. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from the shrine in battle, he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. He was beheaded on [[July 23]] [[1335]].
The cave still exists today in the rockface behind the shrine, and is a tourist attraction. It is four meters deep and has an area of 12 square meters.
==References== * [http://www.asahi-net.or.jp/~qm9t-kndu/kamakugu.htm Kamakura-gū]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Kamakura-gū | image = Kamakuragu Main Hall.jpg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|19|34|N|139|34|0|E|display=title,inline|region:JP-14_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Prince Morinaga]] | founded_by = Meiji Emperor | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = 154 [[Nikaidō]], Kamakura, Kanagawa 248–0002 | website = {{URL|http://www.kamakuraguu.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Kamakura-gū'''|鎌倉宮|}} is a [[Jinja (shrine)|shrine]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It was erected by [[Emperor Meiji]] in 1869 to enshrine the spirit of [[Prince Morinaga]], who was imprisoned and later executed where the shrine now stands in 1335 by order of [[Ashikaga Tadayoshi]]. For this reason, the shrine is also known as {{nihongo|Ōtōnomiya or Daitōnomiya|大塔宮}} from the Prince's full name (Ōtōnomiya Morinaga).
Prince Morinaga was [[Ashikaga Takauji]]'s most dangerous political rival in Kyoto, so he was arrested with a pretext by him in 1334 and first kept prisoner there, then had him sent to Kamakura. Ashikaga's younger brother Tadayoshi held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura-gū. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from Kamakura after losing a battle to [[Hōjō Tokiyuki]], before leaving he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. The Prince was beheaded on July 23, 1335. The cave still exists today in the rockface behind the shrine, and is a tourist attraction. It is four meters deep and has an area of 12 square meters.
It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==See also== * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== * {{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan (3-volume boxed set) |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co. |date=January 1, 1977 |edition=2000 |volume=2 |isbn=4-8053-0375-1 }}
{{Shinto shrine}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Kamakura-gū | image = Kamakuragu Main Hall.jpg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|19|34|N|139|34|0|E|display=title,inline|region:JP-14_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Prince Morinaga]] | founded_by = Meiji Emperor | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = 154 [[Nikaidō]], Kamakura, Kanagawa 248–0002 | website = {{URL|http://www.kamakuraguu.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Kamakura-gū'''|鎌倉宮|}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was erected by [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] in 1869 to enshrine the spirit of [[Prince Morinaga]], who was imprisoned and later executed where the shrine now stands in 1335 by order of [[:en:Ashikaga Tadayoshi|Ashikaga Tadayoshi]]. For this reason, the shrine is also known as {{nihongo|Ōtōnomiya or Daitōnomiya|大塔宮}} from the Prince's full name (Ōtōnomiya Morinaga).
Prince Morinaga was [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]'s most dangerous political rival in Kyoto, so he was arrested with a pretext by him in 1334 and first kept prisoner there, then had him sent to Kamakura. Ashikaga's younger brother Tadayoshi held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura-gū. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from Kamakura after losing a battle to [[Hōjō Tokiyuki]], before leaving he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. The Prince was beheaded on July 23, 1335. The cave still exists today in the rockface behind the shrine, and is a tourist attraction. It is four meters deep and has an area of 12 square meters.
It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== * {{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan (3-volume boxed set) |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co. |date=January 1, 1977 |edition=2000 |volume=2 |isbn=4-8053-0375-1 }}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Kamakura-gū | image = Kamakuragu Main Hall.jpg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|19|34|N|139|34|0|E|display=title,inline|region:JP-14_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Prince Morinaga]] | founded_by = Meiji Emperor | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = 154 [[Nikaidō]], Kamakura, Kanagawa 248–0002 | website = {{URL|http://www.kamakuraguu.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Kamakura-gū'''|鎌倉宮|}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was erected by [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] in 1869 to enshrine the spirit of [[Prince Morinaga]], who was imprisoned and later executed where the shrine now stands in 1335 by order of [[:en:Ashikaga Tadayoshi|Ashikaga Tadayoshi]]. For this reason, the shrine is also known as {{nihongo|Ōtōnomiya or Daitōnomiya|大塔宮}} from the Prince's full name (Ōtōnomiya Morinaga).
Prince Morinaga was [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]'s most dangerous political rival in Kyoto, so he was arrested with a pretext by him in 1334 and first kept prisoner there, then had him sent to Kamakura. Ashikaga's younger brother Tadayoshi held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura-gū. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from Kamakura after losing a battle to [[Hōjō Tokiyuki]], before leaving he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. The Prince was beheaded on July 23, 1335. The cave still exists today in the rockface behind the shrine, and is a tourist attraction. It is four meters deep and has an area of 12 square meters.
It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== * {{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan (3-volume boxed set) |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co. |date=January 1, 1977 |edition=2000 |volume=2 |isbn=4-8053-0375-1 }}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q710351]] Wikidata name: Kamakura-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q710351]] Wikidata name: Kamakura-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamakura-gū | image = Kamakuragu Main Hall.jpg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|19|34|N|139|34|0|E|display=title,inline|region:JP-14_type:landmark_source:dewiki}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[:en:Prince Morinaga|Prince Morinaga]] | founded_by = Meiji Emperor | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = 154 [[:en:Nikaidō|Nikaidō]], Kamakura, Kanagawa 248–0002 | website = {{URL|http://www.kamakuraguu.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Kamakura-gū'''|鎌倉宮|}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was erected by [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] in 1869 to enshrine the spirit of [[:en:Prince Morinaga|Prince Morinaga]], who was imprisoned and later executed where the shrine now stands in 1335 by order of [[:en:Ashikaga Tadayoshi|Ashikaga Tadayoshi]]. For this reason, the shrine is also known as {{nihongo|Ōtōnomiya or Daitōnomiya|大塔宮}} from the Prince's full name (Ōtōnomiya Morinaga).
Prince Morinaga was [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]'s most dangerous political rival in Kyoto, so he was arrested with a pretext by him in 1334 and first kept prisoner there, then had him sent to Kamakura. Ashikaga's younger brother Tadayoshi held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura-gū. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from Kamakura after losing a battle to [[Hōjō Tokiyuki]], before leaving he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. The Prince was beheaded on July 23, 1335. The cave still exists today in the rockface behind the shrine, and is a tourist attraction. It is four meters deep and has an area of 12 square meters.
It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== * {{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan (3-volume boxed set) |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co. |date=January 1, 1977 |edition=2000 |volume=2 |isbn=4-8053-0375-1 }}

It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[arz:كاماكورا جو]] [[de:Kamakura-gū]] [[fr:Kamakura-gū]] [[it:Kamakura-gu]] [[ja:鎌倉宮]] [[zh:鎌倉宮]] {{wikidata link|Q710351}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كاماكورا جو]][[de:Kamakura-gū]][[en:Kamakura-gū]][[fr:Kamakura-gū]][[it:Kamakura-gu]][[ja:鎌倉宮]][[zh:鎌倉宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q710351]] Wikidata name: Kamakura-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q710351]] Wikidata name: Kamakura-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamakura-gū | image = File:Kamakuragu Main Hall.jpg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.32611|N|139.56666|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Moriyoshi-shinnō|WD=Q2288858}} | founded_by = Meiji Emperor | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = 154 [[:en:Nikaidō|Nikaidō]], Kamakura, Kanagawa 248–0002 | website = {{URL|http://www.kamakuraguu.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Nikaidō|WD=Q21654018}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Kamakura-gū'''|鎌倉宮|}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was erected by [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] in 1869 to enshrine the spirit of [[:en:Prince Morinaga|Prince Morinaga]], who was imprisoned and later executed where the shrine now stands in 1335 by order of [[:en:Ashikaga Tadayoshi|Ashikaga Tadayoshi]]. For this reason, the shrine is also known as {{nihongo|Ōtōnomiya or Daitōnomiya|大塔宮}} from the Prince's full name (Ōtōnomiya Morinaga).
Prince Morinaga was [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]'s most dangerous political rival in Kyoto, so he was arrested with a pretext by him in 1334 and first kept prisoner there, then had him sent to Kamakura. Ashikaga's younger brother Tadayoshi held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura-gū. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from Kamakura after losing a battle to [[Hōjō Tokiyuki]], before leaving he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. The Prince was beheaded on July 23, 1335. The cave still exists today in the rockface behind the shrine, and is a tourist attraction. It is four meters deep and has an area of 12 square meters.
It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]

It was erected by [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] in 1869 to enshrine the spirit of [[:en:Prince Morinaga|Prince Morinaga]], who was imprisoned and later executed where the shrine now stands in 1335 by order of [[:en:Ashikaga Tadayoshi|Ashikaga Tadayoshi]]. For this reason, the shrine is also known as {{nihongo|Ōtōnomiya or Daitōnomiya|大塔宮}} from the Prince's full name (Ōtōnomiya Morinaga).
Prince Morinaga was [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]'s most dangerous political rival in Kyoto, so he was arrested with a pretext by him in 1334 and first kept prisoner there, then had him sent to Kamakura. Ashikaga's younger brother Tadayoshi held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura-gū. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from Kamakura after losing a battle to [[Hōjō Tokiyuki]], before leaving he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. The Prince was beheaded on July 23, 1335. The cave still exists today in the rockface behind the shrine, and is a tourist attraction. It is four meters deep and has an area of 12 square meters.
It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== * {{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan (3-volume boxed set) |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co. |date=January 1, 1977 |edition=2000 |volume=2 |isbn=4-8053-0375-1 }}

It was erected by [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] in 1869 to enshrine the spirit of [[:en:Prince Morinaga|Prince Morinaga]], who was imprisoned and later executed where the shrine now stands in 1335 by order of [[:en:Ashikaga Tadayoshi|Ashikaga Tadayoshi]]. For this reason, the shrine is also known as {{nihongo|Ōtōnomiya or Daitōnomiya|大塔宮}} from the Prince's full name (Ōtōnomiya Morinaga).
Prince Morinaga was [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]'s most dangerous political rival in Kyoto, so he was arrested with a pretext by him in 1334 and first kept prisoner there, then had him sent to Kamakura. Ashikaga's younger brother Tadayoshi held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura-gū. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from Kamakura after losing a battle to [[Hōjō Tokiyuki]], before leaving he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. The Prince was beheaded on July 23, 1335. revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كاماكورا جو]][[de:Kamakura-gū]][[en:Kamakura-gū]][[fr:Kamakura-gū]][[it:Kamakura-gu]][[ja:鎌倉宮]][[zh:鎌倉宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كاماكورا جو]][[de:Kamakura-gū]][[en:Kamakura-gū]][[fr:Kamakura-gū]][[it:Kamakura-gu]][[ja:鎌倉宮]][[zh:鎌倉宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كاماكورا جو]][[de:Kamakura-gū]][[en:Kamakura-gū]][[fr:Kamakura-gū]][[it:Kamakura-gu]][[ja:鎌倉宮]][[zh:鎌倉宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كاماكورا جو]][[de:Kamakura-gū]][[en:Kamakura-gū]][[fr:Kamakura-gū]][[it:Kamakura-gu]][[ja:鎌倉宮]][[zh:鎌倉宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q710351]] Wikidata name: Kamakura-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q710351]] Wikidata name: Kamakura-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamakura-gū | image = File:Kamakuragu Main Hall.jpg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.32611|N|139.56666|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Moriyoshi-shinnō|WD=Q2288858}} | founded_by = Meiji Emperor | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = 154 {{ill|Nikaidō|en|Nikaidō|WD=Q21654018|lt=Nikaidō}}, Kamakura, Kanagawa 248–0002 | website = {{URL|http://www.kamakuraguu.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Nikaidō|WD=Q21654018}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Kamakura-gū'''|鎌倉宮|}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}} in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It was erected by {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} in 1869 to enshrine the spirit of [[:en:Prince Morinaga|Prince Morinaga]], who was imprisoned and later executed where the shrine now stands in 1335 by order of {{ill|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|en|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|WD=Q725375|lt=Ashikaga Tadayoshi}}. For this reason, the shrine is also known as {{nihongo|Ōtōnomiya or Daitōnomiya|大塔宮}} from the Prince's full name (Ōtōnomiya Morinaga).
Prince Morinaga was {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}}'s most dangerous political rival in Kyoto, so he was arrested with a pretext by him in 1334 and first kept prisoner there, then had him sent to Kamakura. Ashikaga's younger brother Tadayoshi held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura-gū. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from Kamakura after losing a battle to [[Hōjō Tokiyuki]], before leaving he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. The Prince was beheaded on July 23, 1335. The cave still exists today in the rockface behind the shrine, and is a tourist attraction. It is four meters deep and has an area of 12 square meters.
It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== * {{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan (3-volume boxed set) |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co. |date=January 1, 1977 |edition=2000 |volume=2 |isbn=4-8053-0375-1 }}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كاماكورا جو]][[de:Kamakura-gū]][[en:Kamakura-gū]][[fr:Kamakura-gū]][[it:Kamakura-gu]][[ja:鎌倉宮]][[zh:鎌倉宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كاماكورا جو]][[de:Kamakura-gū]][[en:Kamakura-gū]][[fr:Kamakura-gū]][[it:Kamakura-gu]][[ja:鎌倉宮]][[zh:鎌倉宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كاماكورا جو]][[de:Kamakura-gū]][[en:Kamakura-gū]][[fr:Kamakura-gū]][[it:Kamakura-gu]][[ja:鎌倉宮]][[zh:鎌倉宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كاماكورا جو]][[de:Kamakura-gū]][[en:Kamakura-gū]][[fr:Kamakura-gū]][[it:Kamakura-gu]][[ja:鎌倉宮]][[zh:鎌倉宮]]
{{Infobox religious building | name = Kamakura-gū | image = File:Kamakuragu Main Hall.jpg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.32611|N|139.56666|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Moriyoshi-shinnō|WD=Q2288858}} | founded_by = Meiji Emperor | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = 154 {{ill|Nikaidō|en|Nikaidō|WD=Q21654018|lt=Nikaidō}}, Kamakura, Kanagawa 248–0002 | website = {{URL|http://www.kamakuraguu.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Nikaidō|WD=Q21654018}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Kamakura-gū'''|鎌倉宮|}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine}} in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It was erected by {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} in 1869 to enshrine the spirit of [[:en:Prince Morinaga|Prince Morinaga]], who was imprisoned and later executed where the shrine now stands in 1335 by order of {{ill|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|en|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|WD=Q725375|lt=Ashikaga Tadayoshi}}. For this reason, the shrine is also known as {{nihongo|Ōtōnomiya or Daitōnomiya|大塔宮}} from the Prince's full name (Ōtōnomiya Morinaga).
Prince Morinaga was {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}}'s most dangerous political rival in Kyoto, so he was arrested with a pretext by him in 1334 and first kept prisoner there, then had him sent to Kamakura. Ashikaga's younger brother Tadayoshi held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura-gū. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from Kamakura after losing a battle to [[Hōjō Tokiyuki]], before leaving he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. The Prince was beheaded on July 23, 1335. The cave still exists today in the rockface behind the shrine, and is a tourist attraction. It is four meters deep and has an area of 12 square meters.
It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== * {{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan (3-volume boxed set) |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co. |date=January 1, 1977 |edition=2000 |volume=2 |isbn=4-8053-0375-1 }}

For this reason, the shrine is also known as {{nihongo|Ōtōnomiya or Daitōnomiya|大塔宮}} from the Prince's full name (Ōtōnomiya Morinaga).

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كاماكورا جو]][[de:Kamakura-gū]][[en:Kamakura-gū]][[fr:Kamakura-gū]][[it:Kamakura-gu]][[ja:鎌倉宮]][[zh:鎌倉宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كاماكورا جو]][[de:Kamakura-gū]][[en:Kamakura-gū]][[fr:Kamakura-gū]][[it:Kamakura-gu]][[ja:鎌倉宮]][[zh:鎌倉宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كاماكورا جو]][[de:Kamakura-gū]][[en:Kamakura-gū]][[fr:Kamakura-gū]][[it:Kamakura-gu]][[ja:鎌倉宮]][[zh:鎌倉宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:كاماكورا جو]][[de:Kamakura-gū]][[en:Kamakura-gū]][[fr:Kamakura-gū]][[it:Kamakura-gu]][[ja:鎌倉宮]][[zh:鎌倉宮]]
{{Infobox religious building | name = Kamakura-gū | image = File:Kamakuragu Main Hall.jpg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.32611|N|139.56666|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Moriyoshi-shinnō|WD=Q2288858|1=Prince Moriyoshi}} | founded_by = Meiji Emperor | established = 1869 | date_destroyed = | location = 154 {{ill|Nikaidō|en|Nikaidō|WD=Q21654018|lt=Nikaidō|1=Nikaidō}}, Kamakura, Kanagawa 248–0002 | website = {{URL|http://www.kamakuraguu.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Nikaidō|WD=Q21654018|1=Nikaidō}} }} {{Nihongo|'''Kamakura-gū'''|鎌倉宮|}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It was erected by {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} in 1869 to enshrine the spirit of [[:en:Prince Morinaga|Prince Morinaga]], who was imprisoned and later executed where the shrine now stands in 1335 by order of {{ill|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|en|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|WD=Q725375|lt=Ashikaga Tadayoshi|1=Ashikaga Tadayoshi}}. For this reason, the shrine is also known as {{nihongo|Ōtōnomiya or Daitōnomiya|大塔宮}} from the Prince's full name (Ōtōnomiya Morinaga).
Prince Morinaga was {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}'s most dangerous political rival in Kyoto, so he was arrested with a pretext by him in 1334 and first kept prisoner there, then had him sent to Kamakura. Ashikaga's younger brother Tadayoshi held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura-gū. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from Kamakura after losing a battle to [[Hōjō Tokiyuki]], before leaving he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. The Prince was beheaded on July 23, 1335. The cave still exists today in the rockface behind the shrine, and is a tourist attraction. It is four meters deep and has an area of 12 square meters.
It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
==References== * {{cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan (3-volume boxed set) |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co. |date=January 1, 1977 |edition=2000 |volume=2 |isbn=4-8053-0375-1 }}

It was erected by {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} in 1869 to enshrine the spirit of [[:en:Prince Morinaga|Prince Morinaga]], who was imprisoned and later executed where the shrine now stands in 1335 by order of {{ill|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|en|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|qid=Q725375|lt=Ashikaga Tadayoshi|1=Ashikaga Tadayoshi}}. For this reason, the shrine is also known as {{nihongo|Ōtōnomiya or Daitōnomiya|大塔宮}} from the Prince's full name (Ōtōnomiya Morinaga).
Prince Morinaga was {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|qid=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}'s most dangerous political rival in Kyoto, so he was arrested with a pretext by him in 1334 and first kept prisoner there, then had him sent to Kamakura. Ashikaga's younger brother Tadayoshi held Morinaga captive for nine months in a small cave at the site of the present Kamakura-gū. When Tadayoshi was forced to retreat from Kamakura after losing a battle to [[Hōjō Tokiyuki]], before leaving he gave the order for Morinaga's execution. The Prince was beheaded on July 23, 1335. The cave still exists today in the rockface behind the shrine, and is a tourist attraction. It is four meters deep and has an area of 12 square meters.
It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==See also== * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]
